reasoning: Confirmed. The sink at lines 321-329 of class-cei-core.php is fully reachable with attacker-controlled input. Attack path: (1) Authenticated user with edit_theme_options capability (the actual gate in init(), NOT install_plugins as stated in the finding — install_plugins is only referenced in the dead can_import() helper that is never called on the import path) accesses the WP Customizer; (2) Uploads a crafted .dat file containing a PHP serialized array whose 'options' key maps arbitrary WP option names to attacker-chosen values; (3) The file type check (wp_check_filetype) only inspects the client-supplied filename extension, not actual MIME, and is trivially bypassed by naming the file *.dat; (4) unserialize() uses allowed_classes=>false (PHP 7+ safe mode), so no PHP object injection — but array keys survive intact; (5) The import loop iterates $data['options'] and for each entry constructs new CEI_Option($wp_customize, $option_key, ...) then calls ->import($option_value), which calls WP_Customize_Setting::update(), which calls update_option($option_key, $option_value) with the key taken verbatim from the attacker file; (6) No allowlist, no exclusion list (self::$core_options is export-only, confirmed by grep — the in_array check at line 203 is inside _export(), absent from _import()), no sanitize_key, no capability check per option. Writable targets include siteurl, admin_email, users_can_register, default_role, active_plugins, enabling full site takeover. Reachability note: the finding claims install_plugins capability is required, but the actual code gate is edit_theme_options (line 58), which in non-default WP configurations can be granted to editors. In default WP this is admin-only, making it admin-to-admin escalation beyond customizer scope — still a real security boundary violation. Severity HIGH is correct.
